Renovation · Trim work · Hallway

I sketched it on paper and then I built it — the faux column hallway upgrade

Marcie  ·  July 2021  ·  7 min read

Every project I do starts with a moment. A pause. A tilt of the head. A thought that sounds like: this has so much potential.

This one started in our hallway. The wall between the front door and the corridor leading back to the kitchen was just that — a wall. Flat, blank, functional. No detail, no character, nothing to announce that you had arrived somewhere worth arriving. The front door was beautiful, the rooms beyond were getting there, but this transition space was doing absolutely nothing.

So I got out a notebook and started drawing.

Before
After

The same wall. What changed is entirely built from trim boards, MDF, and the willingness to draw it on paper first.

Starting with a sketch

The plan — drawn by hand, built by hand

This is the actual sketch I made before touching a single piece of wood. You can see the column, the decorative panel insets, the rug labeled below. I drew the hallway in perspective so I could see how it would feel to walk through it. That sketch became the blueprint. Everything in the final photos was in this drawing first.

I want to spend a moment on this because I think it matters. I am not an architect. I didn't use software or a drafting table. I sat at my kitchen table with a notebook and a pen and I drew what I wanted to see. Then I figured out how to build it.

Drawing it first forces you to commit to a decision before you've spent any money on lumber. It also lets you see the proportions — is the column too wide? Too short? Is the panel too centered or does it need to sit lower? I adjusted the sketch three times before I was happy with it. That took ten minutes and cost nothing. The alternative is buying the wrong lumber and finding out on the wall.

"Drawing it first forces you to commit to a decision before you've spent any money. It lets you see the proportions, catch the problems, and fix them in ink instead of wood."

What the before really looked like

The hallway before — a flat beige corridor with basic door casing and nothing more. Every surface was crying out for some architecture.

The before photo tells the whole story. Basic flat door casing, plain beige walls, no detail between floor and ceiling. The hallway was purely functional — a passageway, not a place. The front door had already been upgraded with crystal knob hardware, which made the surrounding walls feel even more unfinished by contrast. When one element of a space gets better, the rest of the space starts to look worse. That's usually the sign it's time to act.

The build — step by step

1
Build the column box
The faux column is built around the existing wall corner — it's essentially a hollow box of MDF that wraps the corner and creates the illusion of a structural column. I built the box from 3/4" MDF, cut to the width I wanted the column face to be, and glued and nailed it directly to the wall. A cap piece at the top (with a simple ogee profile router detail) and a base block at the floor created the column's top and bottom. The key is making the column proportionally correct — too thin and it reads as a box, too wide and it dominates the space. I used my sketch proportions as the guide.
2
Add the decorative panel detail
This is the detail that makes the whole thing. The decorative inset panels — the ones with the Greek key corner detail you can see in the final photos — are built up from multiple layers of flat trim. The outer frame is a simple square of flat casing. Inside that, a second layer of thinner trim creates the reveal. The corner detail (that small notched Greek key shape) is built up from small square offcuts of MDF, mitered and stacked to create the dimensional corner accent. Every piece is glued and nailed, then filled and sanded smooth before paint. The panel detail continues along the full hallway wall, not just the column face, so the whole space reads as unified.
3
Run the wainscoting down the full hallway
The column is the feature but it only works because the panel treatment continues down the hallway wall on both sides. This is the detail that transforms a single accent piece into an architectural system. I ran a cap rail at chair-rail height all the way down the corridor, and added the same panel-style detail below it on both sides. The panels vary slightly in width to fit the available wall sections, but the style — the layered frame with the Greek key corner accent — is consistent throughout. This is what makes a hallway feel like it belongs to an old house rather than a house that's trying to look old.
4
Caulk, fill, paint
Every joint caulked. Every nail hole filled. Every seam sanded. Two coats of semi-gloss white. The paint step is always where the build becomes real — where the raw MDF and tape marks disappear and you see the finished thing for the first time. I always feel a combination of relief and satisfaction at this stage. And a little bit of "I can't believe that worked."

The full hallway — before and after

The hallway now reads as a proper architectural sequence — the column marks the transition from entry to interior, and the panel detail gives every surface something to say.

"The hallway now reads as a proper architectural sequence. The column marks the transition from entry to interior — it announces you've arrived somewhere."

Why this project matters beyond the aesthetics

A hallway is the first thing you see when you walk through the front door. It sets the tone for everything that follows. When it's flat and beige and purely functional, that tone is "nothing interesting happens here." When it has architectural detail — a column that marks a transition, panels that give the walls rhythm and depth — that tone changes entirely.

This project cost almost nothing in materials. MDF is inexpensive. Trim boards are inexpensive. Paint is inexpensive. What it costs is time and the willingness to plan carefully before you cut. The sketch did most of the work. The saw just made it real.

The Greek key corner detail — how it's done

Cut small squares of 1/4" MDF in three sizes: outer, middle, and inner. Stack them at each corner of the panel frame, each one centered on the previous, to create the stepped notch profile. Glue down with wood glue, then pin nail. Fill any gaps with caulk before painting. The whole corner detail takes about 20 minutes per panel and is the single most impactful thing you can do to elevate basic board-and-frame wainscoting into something that looks custom.

What I used

  • 3/4" MDF sheet goods (column box and panel frames)
  • 1/4" MDF (layered panel detail and Greek key corners)
  • Flat door casing profile (outer panel frame)
  • Thinner screen bead moulding (inner reveal)
  • Cap rail moulding (chair-rail height throughout)
  • Baseboard moulding (new throughout hallway)
  • Brad nailer + 1.5" and 2" brad nails
  • Wood glue + Liquid Nails
  • Paintable latex caulk
  • Wood filler + sandpaper (120 and 220 grit)
  • Semi-gloss white paint — 2 coats
